title: GDPR Data Subject Access Request Fulfillment Tracking
exclusions: Requests withdrawn by the data subject before processing begins; requests correctly refused under Art.12(5) as manifestly unfounded or excessive with documented refusal justification.
evidence: DSAR fulfilment log with timestamps for receipt, identity verification, extraction, review, and delivery; SLA compliance percentage calculated monthly and archived for accountability demonstration.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Create a test DSAR in the ticketing system with a known creation timestamp; allow the SLA clock to approach threshold (e.g., 25 of 30 days); verify the alert fires and the dashboard shows the request as at-risk before the deadline.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Create a test DSAR and mark it as completed within 5 business days; verify no SLA breach alert fires and the dashboard shows the request as fulfilled with green status.

dataSources: `index=itsm` `sourcetype="snow:sc_req_item"` (number, cat_item, opened_at, closed_at, state, short_description) or `sourcetype="snow:incident"` (number, category, opened_at, closed_at, short_description, priority)
spl:
index=itsm (sourcetype="snow:sc_req_item" OR sourcetype="snow:incident")
    (cat_item="*Subject Access*" OR short_description="*DSAR*" OR short_description="*data subject*")
| eval opened_epoch=strptime(opened_at, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval closed_epoch=if(isnotnull(closed_at), strptime(closed_at, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"), null())
| eval age_days=round((now()-opened_epoch)/86400, 1)
| eval sla_met=if(isnotnull(closed_epoch) AND (closed_epoch-opened_epoch)<=2592000, "Met", "Missed")
| eval open_breach=if(isnull(closed_epoch) AND age_days>30, "Open_SLA_Breach", null())
| table _time, number, sourcetype, state, age_days, sla_met, open_breach, short_description
| sort - age_days
description: Measures DSAR ticket lifecycle from opened to closed against a 30-calendar-day SLA so privacy and audit teams can evidence timely handling of access, rectification, erasure, and portability requests.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes itsm and sourcetypes snow:incident, snow:sc_req_item. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Install Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (1928) with sc_req_item and incident inputs enabled; (2) align `cat_item`/`short_description` filters with your DSAR catalogue naming; (3) confirm timestamp format in `opened_at`/`closed_at` and adjust `strptime` format if needed; (4) schedule daily and alert on `open_breach`. Indexes required: `index=itsm`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=snow:incident`, `sourcetype=snow:sc_req_item`.
visualization: Column chart (Met vs Missed), Time chart (DSAR volume), Table (open breaches), Single value (% within 30 days). Key fields: `age_days`, `closed_epoch`, `number`, `open_breach`, `opened_epoch`, `short_description`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Multi-system extraction delays** — DSARs that span legacy systems with batch-only interfaces may show extraction times exceeding SLA thresholds; verify the DSAR ticket contains an approved extension documented under Art.12(3) before treating as a genuine breach.

2. **Vendor-managed system coordination** — Third-party processor responses may arrive outside the controller's normal business hours, creating apparent SLA violations that resolve once the processor's response is ingested into the tracking system.

3. **Complex identity verification cycles** — DSARs requiring additional identity verification under Art.12(6) pause the response clock; the search may flag the overall elapsed time without accounting for the verification hold period.

4. **Bulk DSAR campaigns** — Organised campaigns (e.g., privacy advocacy groups) may generate dozens of simultaneous requests that temporarily exceed capacity, triggering SLA alerts that are already being managed under the manifestly excessive provision of Art.12(5).

5. **Withdrawn requests** — Data subjects who voluntarily withdraw their DSAR before completion may leave open tickets that appear as unfulfilled requests; check the ticket status for withdrawal confirmation.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`gdpr_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `itsm` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.15`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.15 (Right of access) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.2: GDPR Data Subject Access Request Fulfillment Tracking." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928), landing events in `itsm` under `snow:sc_req_item`, `snow:incident`. REST-polled ticket tables produce 500-5000 records per sync per table; at 300s interval expect ~3-8 GB/month for a mid-size service desk.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > ServiceNow Service Account. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[snow://{instance_slug}]
url = https://<instance>.service-now.com
table = {table_name}
interval = 300
index = itsm
sourcetype = {sourcetype}
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `number, state, priority, assignment_group, opened_at, closed_at, sys_updated_on, caller_id, category, subcategory`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=itsm earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Install Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** with sc_req_item and incident inputs enabled. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** align `cat_item`/`short_description` filters with your DSAR catalogue naming. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** confirm timestamp format in `opened_at`/`closed_at` and adjust `strptime` format if needed. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** schedule daily and alert on `open_breach`. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "GDPR Data Subject Access Request Fulfillment Tracking," the aggregation summarises access activity — the control is about WHO accessed WHAT, so the key fields in the aggregate map directly to 'principal' and 'resource' for the compliance narrative.

This detection correlates event arrival time with a business timestamp from `opened_at` (format: `%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S`). The `strptime()` conversion to epoch is the linchpin — the subsequent `where` clause compares Splunk's `_time` against the parsed epoch to detect policy violations (activity after a termination, events past a retention boundary, operations outside a maintenance window, etc).

Silent failure mode: if `opened_at` arrives in a format the `strptime` call doesn't match, the conversion returns null, propagates through the `where` comparison as 'unknown,' and the row is dropped from results. This produces under-reporting, not over-reporting — which is the dangerous direction for a compliance detection. Validate by running:

```spl
<base search>
| eval opened_at_epoch = strptime(opened_at, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| where isnull(opened_at_epoch)
| stats count by opened_at
```

If this returns rows, the upstream system has multiple timestamp formats (common with SaaS connectors that change during version upgrades) and the `strptime` call must be hardened with `coalesce(strptime(opened_at, "fmt1"), strptime(opened_at, "fmt2"))` to handle them all.

Time-zone alignment: if the source system emits `opened_at` in a different zone from the Splunk search head, the comparison will be offset. Look for `%z` or `%Z` in the format string — if absent, the conversion uses `TZ = TZ of Splunk host`, which is correct only when source and target agree. Search covers the full search range.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.1.2: GDPR Data Subject Access Request Fulfillment Tracking]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.1.2,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sc_req_item earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `_time`, `age_days`, `closed_epoch`, `number`, `open_breach`, `opened_epoch`, `short_description`, `sla_met`, `sourcetype`, `state`. Run:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sc_req_item earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table _time, age_days, closed_epoch, number, open_breach, opened_epoch, short_description, sla_met
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `age_days`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(age_days) as p50 perc90(age_days) as p90 perc99(age_days) as p99 max(age_days) as hi
```

The current threshold `30` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.1.2: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Distribution (`Met vs Missed`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.

**Panel 2 — Time trend (`DSAR volume`):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.

**Panel 3 — Drill-through table (`open breaches`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 4 — Single-value KPI (`% within 30 days`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'GDPR current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Silent under-reporting:** The `strptime` conversion is likely failing on a subset of events — see Step 3 for the hardening procedure. Check for format drift after upstream system upgrades.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We measure DSAR ticket lifecycle from opened to closed against a 30-calendar-day SLA so privacy and audit teams can evidence timely handling of access, rectification, erasure, and portability requests.
